name: Burt Bacharach
abstract: Burt Freeman Bacharach ( BAK-ə-rak; May 12, 1928 – February 8, 2023) was an American composer, songwriter, record producer, and pianist, widely regarded as one of the most important and influential figures of 20th-century popular music. A significant figure in orchestral pop and easy listening, he composed hundreds of songs, many in coll…

about_quotes.speaker: Chuck Findley
about_quotes.evidence: Well Burt Bacharach wrote it... on that session there were three trumpets. It was myself... And Burt Bacharach of course, who wrote the song, I don’t know if you know that Burt was very particular with what he wanted.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chuck Findley interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, September 6, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Steve Allen
about_quotes.evidence: another one who then showed up was Burt Bacharach, who gave us about a dozen of the best songs ever written.
about_quotes.doc_title: Steve Allen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Larry Gold
about_quotes.evidence: I think Tommy Bell was enamored with Burt Bacharach. And Burt Bacharach was very sophisticated you know?
about_quotes.doc_title: Larry Gold interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 18, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Dave McKenna
about_quotes.evidence: Certain tunes of Burt Bacharach, “The Look of Love” and all that, we had to play that.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave McKenna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Marshall
about_quotes.evidence: I mean Burt Bacharach wrote beautiful songs for Dionne... I loved playing that music.
about_quotes.doc_title: Eddie Marshall interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Francisco, California, August 8, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Toots Thielemans
about_quotes.evidence: You listen to the YouTube again when he plays… plays Alfie with Burt Bacharach.
about_quotes.doc_title: Toots Thielemans — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
